title: Pterocarpus gracilis
taxonomy: Pterocarpus gracilis is a species within the family Leguminosae, commonly known as the pea family or Fabaceae. It is a genus of flowering plants that is native to tropical and subtropical regions. This plant is further classified into the subfamily Faboideae. The species is also known to be part of the tribe Dalbergieae, which comprises several other species of legumes. Further classification is provided by its distribution in the tropical regions of Asia and the Pacific.
uses: Pterocarpus gracilis, also known as the forest mahogany, is used in traditional medicine. The bark and leaves of the tree are believed to have various health benefits, including treating fever and gastrointestinal issues. Pterocarpus gracilis is also used to treat various skin problems and to reduce inflammation. Its antiseptic properties make it useful in treating wounds and infections. The medicinal uses of Pterocarpus gracilis are primarily based on its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties.

The plant has been used in traditional medicine to treat various conditions such as fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems. intro: Pterocarpus gracilis, also known as the forest red or yellowwood, is a deciduous tree species native to tropical Africa. The health benefits of Pterocarpus gracilis include its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which have been traditionally used to treat various ailments such as fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional African medicine, Pterocarpus gracilis is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or ointment, and is commonly used to make teas, tinctures, and topical creams to harness its therapeutic benefits.
